+
+/* atomicAddSingleWriter(var, delta, newvalue_var)
+ *
+ * Adds `delta` to `var` and writes the resulting value to `newvalue_var`.
+ * Same end result as atomicIncrGet() but implemented as load+add+store instead
+ * of an atomic read-modify-write. This avoids the `lock` prefix on x86
+ * (~20-40 cycles vs ~2-3 for plain load+store).
+ *
+ * SAFETY: the caller MUST guarantee that no other thread ever writes to `var`
+ * (no atomicIncr, no atomicSet, no other call to this macro from a different
+ * thread). Concurrent writers cause silent lost updates. Readers on other
+ * threads using atomicGet are fine: they will observe either the pre or
+ * post update value. */
+#define atomicAddSingleWriter(var, delta, newvalue_var) do { \

discussion_comments.comment_body:
Yes, RMW has higher cost as CPU needs to fetch that cache line exclusively and flush the change immediately after incrementing it. On the other hand, load+store+write can do that lazily. On x86, load+store+write does not even emit an atomic operation: https://godbolt.org/z/ePGKeaasz

> now this function seems not an atomic operation, but has atomic prefix, a bit confusing. maybe write them directly

It's an atomic operation if you don't break the contract. I thought macro name + comment makes this obvious but if you still find that confusing, I can move it somewhere else with another name.

discussion_comments.comment_body:
renamed the macro.

Yes, we can use in some other places but not sure where else it does matter. e.g. we may use in lazyfree.c but it is unnecessary to improve performance there.
@oranagra i realized that we can't change it to 16bits(65535), a pubsub channel with 60k subscribers can easily make it reach max refcount.

maybe it's time to finally separate the robj that's used for keyspace values, from the one that's used for general strings (argv, RedisModuleString, pubsub, etc)
this sounds complicated, and could have some sharp corners (places where we wanna 0-copy move them from one to the other).

on the other hand, for small objects (even in the key-space), we don't need a refcount at all, and for large ones, we don't care that the refcount will be 32bit. maybe we can use a mechanism from #14445 for something similar to how we keep the TTL today.

i wonder which of these could be easier to attack first?
maybe just separate the pubsub channel strings to a new string type, and leave the rest of the migration for later?

For reference, here are a few points I raised with @sundb in chat:
* I think maxClients can be beyond 64k
* If we plan to separate kvobj and robj, we should at least start by defining a shared/common header, including refcount.
* We could consider packing the reference count together with the iskvobj and expirable flags into a single atomic variable, allowing us to preserve up to 30 bits for the reference count.
@moticless I use the third suggestion in https://github.com/redis/redis/pull/14608/changes/46b71ecbfc4094746d36db60b917550fd6d94c14

neat.
so since we only increment and decrement the lower bits, while the uppper bits are static, our increments and decrements will never touch the higher bits, and we only need to strip them if we wanna see what the refcount actually is (compare it to 0, or STATIC), and on decr, we strip the bits from the return value of the decr (still atomic).

my only concern is then, if the higher bits are indeed static...
i.e. what would happen if the main thread is trying to change the higher bits (without atomic op), and an IO thread is doing decrement?
i think that'll be an issue.

> my only concern is then, if the higher bits are indeed static... i.e. what would happen if the main thread is trying to change the higher bits (without atomic op), and an IO thread is doing decrement? i think that'll be an issue.

If that's the case, it would be UB, which is also my concern. However, after confirming that expreable and iskvobj are only set at the time of creation, I continued with this approach
Need inline doc concerns and assumptions to make it clear to the reader.
Since the c standard does not stipulate that the bit field at the front of the position must be in the lower bit, I modified it to use bit operations.

commented_code:
#define LRU_CLOCK_RESOLUTION 1000 /* LRU clock resolution in ms */
 
 #define OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS 30
+#define OBJ_REFCOUNT_MASK ((1U << OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS) - 1) /* Mask to extract refcount. */
 #define OBJ_SHARED_REFCOUNT ((1 << OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS) - 1) /* Global object never destroyed. */
 #define OBJ_STATIC_REFCOUNT ((1 << OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS) - 2) /* Object allocated in the stack. */
 #define OBJ_FIRST_SPECIAL_REFCOUNT OBJ_STATIC_REFCOUNT
 
+/* Extract refcount from atomic_flags_refcount. */
+#define OBJ_GET_REFCOUNT(atomic_val) ((atomic_val) & OBJ_REFCOUNT_MASK)
+
 struct redisObject {
     unsigned type:4;
     unsigned encoding:4;
     unsigned lru:LRU_BITS; /* LRU time (relative to global lru_clock) or
-                            * LFU data (least significant 8 bits frequency
-                            * and most significant 16 bits access time). */
-    unsigned iskvobj : 1;   /* 1 if this struct serves as a kvobj base */
-    unsigned expirable : 1; /* 1 if this key has expiration time attached.
-                             * If set, then this object is of type kvobj */
-    unsigned refcount : OBJ_REFCOUNT_BITS;
+                            * LFU data (least significant 16 bits access time). */
+
+    /* Bit layout for atomic_flags_refcount (32 bits total):
+     *   bit 31     (1 bit):   iskvobj   - 1 if this struct serves as a kvobj base
+     *   bit 30     (1 bit):   expirable - 1 if this key has expiration time attached
+     *   bits 0-29  (30 bits): refcount
+     *
+     * The flags (expirable, iskvobj) must be set at creation time and never
+     * modified afterwards. Modifying these bits non-atomically while IO threads
+     * are updating refcount could result in UB. */
+    redisAtomic uint32_t atomic_flags_refcount;
+
     void *ptr;
 };
 
+/* Bit positions for flags in atomic_flags_refcount. */
+#define OBJ_EXPIRABLE_BIT 30
+#define OBJ_ISKVOBJ_BIT 31
+
+/* Access macros for redisObject fields using bitwise operations. */
+#define robj_atomic_flags_refcount(o) ((o)->atomic_flags_refcount)
+
+static inline unsigned int robj_refcount(const robj *o) {
+    uint32_t val;
+    atomicGet(o->atomic_flags_refcount, val);
+    return OBJ_GET_REFCOUNT(val);
+}
+#define robj_expirable(o) (((o)->atomic_flags_refcount >> OBJ_EXPIRABLE_BIT) & 1)
+#define robj_iskvobj(o) (((o)->atomic_flags_refcount >> OBJ_ISKVOBJ_BIT) & 1)
+#define robj_set_refcount(o, val) \

discussion_comments.comment_body:
we call it only when initiating, otherwise it is not safe, so maybe `robj_init_refcount`?

besides, all of accessing atomic variable should  use `atomicGet`, for example, the IO thread may be changing the refcount, but the main thread is calling `robj_expirable`, there is a data race

commented_code:
* node, it should first increase the next node's refcount, and when we trim
  * the replication buffer nodes, we remove node always from the head node which
  * refcount is 0. If the refcount of the head node is not 0, we must stop
- * trimming and never iterate the next node. */
+ * trimming and never iterate the next node.
+ *
+ * For replicas in IO threads we don't update the refcount while sending the
+ * repl data, but only when the thread is send back to main. This avoids data
+ * races. In order to achieve this the replica clients keeps track of following:
+ * - ref_repl_start_node - the node we started to send repl data from
+ * - ref_repl_buf_node - the current node we've reached
+ * - ref_repl_last_node - the last node in the replication buffer as seen by
+ *                        the replica client before it was send to IO thread

discussion_comments.comment_body:
If the client is in main thread, then `ref_repl_start_node` and `ref_repl_buf_node` are the same.
If the client is in IO thread, then `ref_repl_start_node` is the node we should start sending repl data from whereas `ref_repl_buf_node` is the node we've reached. Probably I should rename it to `ref_repl_curr_node`.

If in main thread it's safe to update the `ref_repl_buf_node` refcount, but if in IO-thread we just move the pointer. The refcount is updated only when the client is moved to main thread - that's why we need to keep track of `ref_repl_start_node` so that we know which node's refcounts to update.

I will try to make the comments clearer, thanks for the feedback.
